network.overall_strategy: This network centers on the literature-supported cyanosulfidic and Strecker-accessible chemistry of threonine, using HCN, acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, and threonine aminonitrile as the principal hubs. Hydrothermal environments supply reduced carbon feedstocks such as formate, acetate, pyruvate, and acetaldehyde via Fe-S/mineral-mediated CO2 fixation and carbonyl chemistry, which then feed UV-driven surface cyanosulfidic chemistry in phosphate-buffered pools. Surface pathways generate lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, which converge on threonine aminonitrile and hydantoin intermediates before hydrolysis to threonine/allothreonine mixtures. Additional empirically supported but less mechanistically resolved routes are included as mixture-generating entries (spark discharge, HCN polymer hydrolysate, meteoritic delivery), while a cautious biochemical/prebiotic capture step converts threonine into a dipeptide to address its documented instability. Speculative steps are explicitly labeled where the literature supports principle but not direct threonine demonstration.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, anoxic, H2-rich, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 to formate on Fe-S phases analogous to biological Fe4S4 centers.
reactions.reasoning: Formate generation from CO2 and H2 on greigite/mackinawite under alkaline hydrothermal conditions is a core Fe-S world step and provides reduced C1 feedstock that can move into surface chemistry (White et al., 2015; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Magnetite/greigite-mediated carbon fixation to acetate and pyruvate

conditions.other: subseafloor/alkaline vent carbon fixation under anoxic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation on Fe-bearing mineral surfaces generates small carboxylates including acetate and pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-mineral systems can generate acetate and pyruvate-like products from reduced carbon flux; pyruvate formation from carbonylated Fe-S systems is experimentally supported and underpins later keto-acid chemistry (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type and carbonyl chemistry to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal carbonyl/hydrogenation regime; product transfer to cooler zones required

reactions.mechanism: Reduced carbon pools undergo surface-mediated hydrogenation and carbonyl transformations to small aldehydes including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde is a recognized abundant prebiotic aldehyde in discharge and carbonyl chemistry; hydrothermal mineral-mediated generation is used here as a connecting feedstock source, while acknowledging downstream threonine synthesis must occur in cooler settings because amino acids are heat-labile (Cody et al., 2000; Marshall, 1994; Aubrey et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Surface UV photochemical generation of HCN/formamide pool

conditions.other: UV-exposed evaporitic pools; anoxic; photochemical concentration cycles

reactions.mechanism: UV photochemistry over photocatalytic mineral surfaces supports nitrile/formamide generation from reduced carbon and nitrogen pools.
reactions.reasoning: Surface photochemistry is required to supply nitrile-rich feedstocks for the cyanosulfidic network; TiO2 photocatalysis and UV conversion to formamide/nitrile pools are broadly prebiotically supported, and phosphate-buffered surface pools are consistent with cyanosulfidic settings (Dunn et al., 1981; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox production of glycolaldehyde from HCN

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with Cu(I) cyanide chemistry in solution; mineral environment TiO2 and borate assist photochemistry and sugar stabilization
conditions.other: UV irradiation, H2S reductant, phosphate-buffered shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes reductive homologation through glycolonitrile to glycolaldehyde under UV-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland showed UV photoredox homologation of HCN yields glycolaldehyde precursors using Cu(I) cyanide and H2S; glycolaldehyde is a key branch point linking sugar and amino acid chemistry. Borate support is included because borate stabilizes sugars in surface settings (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Aldol homologation of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry surface cycles; borate stabilizes triose products

reactions.mechanism: Formose-like aldol homologation and sugar equilibration convert glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde under borate-stabilized conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is a documented branch point between nucleotide precursor chemistry and threonine aminonitrile formation; borate minerals stabilize sugars and are central to plausible surface sugar chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Islam et al., 2017).

reactions.name: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin photoreduction to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with Cu(I) cyanide in solution; TiO2 and phosphate-bearing pool setting
conditions.other: H2S reductant; 2 h irradiation; lactaldehyde reported at 19% yield

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms its cyanohydrin with HCN, followed by UV photoreduction of the nitrile-bearing intermediate to lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the key experimentally demonstrated threonine-specific branch: Ritson and Sutherland reported photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde in 19% yield under cyanosulfidic UV conditions (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: phosphate minerals and clay concentration surfaces
conditions.other: aqueous Strecker conditions in evaporitic or hot-spring pools; racemic/diastereomeric aminonitrile expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, then cyanide adds to produce 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde Strecker route is the best-characterized dedicated pathway to threonine precursor formation and is central to the cyanosulfidic network (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: surface cyanosulfidic pool; ribonucleotide precursor chemistry linked to amino acid precursor chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes imine formation with NH3 and cyanide addition to give the same threonine aminonitrile network node.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. explicitly place glyceraldehyde as a parallel route to threonine aminonitrile within cyanosulfidic protometabolism, linking RNA and amino acid precursor synthesis (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis; rapid use favored because threonine is unstable; threonine/allothreonine racemic mixture implied

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide/carboxylate intermediates to yield the amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the terminal step of classical Strecker chemistry; for threonine this produces threonine and allothreonine as racemic mixtures, matching the literature (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of lactaldehyde to hydantoin intermediate

conditions.other: ammonium carbonate/CO2-bearing pool chemistry; hydantoin acts as thermodynamic sink

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide, ammonia, and cyanate/carbonate equivalents to form a substituted hydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. demonstrated Bucherer-Bergs amino acid synthesis via hydantoins for prebiotic keto-acid systems. Applying the same chemistry to lactaldehyde to give 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin is a literature-grounded extension specifically discussed for threonine in the review, though not directly isolated in the cited work; it is therefore plausible but not directly demonstrated for threonine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner and Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin to threonine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in concentrated pools; racemic threonine/allothreonine expected

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin releases the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the core logic of the Bucherer-Bergs pathway; hydantoins in meteorites support prebiotic relevance, though the threonine-specific example remains inferential rather than experimentally isolated (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner and Cooper, 2005).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge mixture route to acetaldehyde, HCN, and threonine

conditions.temperature: spark plasma with collection in ambient aqueous trap
conditions.catalyst: none intrinsic to discharge; mineral shore deposition and concentration by TiO2/clay included as environmental agents
conditions.other: CH4/N2/H2O with traces of NH3, or CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S mixtures; electrical discharge; collected in aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates complex reactive mixtures including HCN and aldehydes; downstream aqueous Strecker chemistry yields trace threonine/allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Ring et al. first confirmed threonine/allothreonine in spark discharge products, and Parker et al. found threonine in H2S-containing archived Miller samples. This reaction is intentionally represented as a heterogeneous mixture-generating entry rather than a mechanistically resolved single transformation (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis to complex amino acid mixture including threonine

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration on clay and UV-active mineral exposure
conditions.other: long-duration oligomerization/polymerization followed by hydrolysis; highly nonselective

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes to nitrile-rich polymers such as DAMN-like species, whose hydrolysis yields broad amino acid mixtures including threonine.
reactions.reasoning: HCN polymer hydrolysates are known to produce threonine among many amino acids, but this is a low-selectivity background source rather than a dedicated route. It is included as such and not overinterpreted (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Aldehyde-ammonia condensation route to hydroxy amino acid family including threonine-type isomers

conditions.other: aqueous aldehyde-ammonia mixtures; product family includes C3/C4 hydroxy amino acid structural isomers

reactions.mechanism: Reactive aldehyde mixtures and ammonia undergo network condensation/rearrangement chemistry yielding hydroxy amino acid families.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka demonstrated that aqueous aldehyde + ammonia chemistry produces hydroxy amino acid families including C3/C4 structural isomers consistent with meteoritic distributions. This route is included cautiously as a family source and not as a selective threonine synthesis claim (Koga and Naraoka, 2022).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine

conditions.other: requires high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance; thermodynamically unfavorable; both threonine and allothreonine expected

reactions.mechanism: Retro-aldol logic is reversed: glycine provides the amino acid nucleophile and acetaldehyde contributes the side-chain carbon unit.
reactions.reasoning: The glycine + acetaldehyde relationship to threonine is chemically well defined from threonine aldolase chemistry, but no purely prebiotic demonstration of this specific condensation is reported; therefore this node is explicitly speculative yet literature-motivated (Fesko, 2016).

reactions.name: Formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from pyruvate and glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: proto-metabolic carbon-carbon addition in concentrated pool; included as a necessary connection to the keto-acid branch

reactions.mechanism: Carbon-carbon bond formation between keto-acid and aldehyde pools generates the β-hydroxy α-keto acid precursor to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The specific prebiotic synthesis of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from pyruvate and glycolaldehyde is not directly demonstrated in the provided literature; this step is therefore speculative but necessary to connect literature-supported α-keto acid reductive amination principles to threonine (Muchowska et al., 2019; Copley et al., 2005).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+ or Ni catalyst in solution
conditions.other: pH 8 for Ni/H2 system; threonine precursor itself not directly tested in cited studies

reactions.mechanism: α-Keto acid undergoes reductive amination with ammonia under Fe2+ or Ni/H2 catalysis to the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids is experimentally established for several substrates with Fe2+ at 70C and Ni/H2 at 22C, but the threonine-specific precursor was not directly tested. This is therefore a principled but extrapolative route and is placed in mild biochemical/prebiotic settings rather than hot hydrothermal conditions because threonine is thermally fragile (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024; Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Meteorite delivery of racemic threonine to surface pools

conditions.catalyst: none for synthesis; minerals act as concentration and preservation surfaces after delivery
conditions.other: delivery from carbonaceous chondrite material such as CR2 GRA 95229

reactions.mechanism: Extraterrestrially synthesized racemic threonine is introduced into prebiotic surface environments where it can join endogenous synthesis networks.
reactions.reasoning: Racemic threonine/allothreonine detected in the pristine CR2 chondrite GRA 95229 provides evidence of abiotic natural synthesis outside Earth; this pathway is delivery rather than terrestrial synthesis but is relevant to connected prebiotic inventories (Pizzarello et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Rapid capture of threonine into dipeptide to mitigate instability

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling; peptide capture as a stabilization strategy for labile threonine

reactions.mechanism: Condensation during dehydration links threonine and glycine to a short peptide, reducing free-threonine residence time.
reactions.reasoning: Because threonine is unusually unstable, rapid incorporation into oligomers is a plausible utilization strategy. Wet-dry mineral-assisted peptide formation is experimentally supported, though not specifically for threonine-glycine in the cited papers (Lahav et al., 1978; Kitadai et al., 2015; Damer and Deamer, 2020; Truong et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon feeds surface HCN chemistry, generating acetaldehyde that is photochemically converted to lactaldehyde and then to threonine aminonitrile before hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Glyceraldehyde parallel Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN homologation generates glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, which directly enters Strecker chemistry to the same threonine aminonitrile hub.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface integrated glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal formate production seeds surface cyanosulfidic homologation, linking vent reductants to RNA/amino acid precursor chemistry and threonine formation.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde enters a hydantoin sink before hydrolysis to threonine, providing an alternative to direct aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge direct mixture route
pathways.description: Electrical discharge in reduced gas mixtures produces complex organic mixtures containing HCN, aldehydes, and detectable threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge to resolved lactaldehyde-Strecker route
pathways.description: Discharge chemistry supplies HCN and acetaldehyde, which then enter the better-resolved cyanosulfidic/lactaldehyde pathway in surface pools.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysate background source
pathways.description: Long-term HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis yields amino acid mixtures including threonine as a low-selectivity reservoir.

pathways.name: Aldehyde-ammonia hydroxy amino acid family route
pathways.description: Mixed aldehydes and ammonia generate hydroxy amino acid families, including threonine-type C4 isomers, in aqueous surface chemistry.

pathways.name: Aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde
pathways.description: A conceptually simple but thermodynamically difficult route joining abundant glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: α-Keto acid reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate and glycolaldehyde feed a speculative but literature-principled keto-acid branch to 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate, followed by reductive amination to threonine under mild conditions.
